identified_error: The survival analysis (Kaplan-Meier and Cox proportional hazards regression) was applied to examine time to resolution of post-COVID-19 symptoms, but the analysis incorrectly treated pre-COVID-19 symptom frequency as a predictor of post-COVID-19 symptom resolution without properly accounting for the fact that children who frequently reported symptoms before COVID-19 likely had chronic or recurring baseline symptoms unrelated to SARS-CoV-2 infection. The hazard ratios for delayed symptom resolution (e.g., HR 0.00 for cough and fatigue) are implausibly extreme and indicate that the model conflated pre-existing chronic/recurring symptoms with post-COVID-19 symptoms, essentially misattributing ongoing baseline symptoms as persistent post-COVID-19 symptoms. The analysis failed to distinguish between symptoms that were continuations of pre-existing conditions and symptoms that were truly caused by SARS-CoV-2 infection.
why_fatal: By not differentiating pre-existing recurring symptoms from genuinely new post-COVID-19 symptoms, the study's central finding — that pre-COVID-19 symptoms predict delayed resolution of post-COVID-19 symptoms — is an artifact of the methodology. Children who already had frequent rhinitis, headache, cough, etc. before infection would naturally continue to report these symptoms after infection, not because of COVID-19 but because of their pre-existing conditions. The implausibly small hazard ratios (0.00-0.08) reflect this confounding. This error invalidates the Cox regression results and the conclusion that pre-COVID-19 symptoms are meaningful risk factors for prolonged post-COVID-19 symptoms. It also potentially affects the PCC incidence estimate of 0.4%, as the definition and attribution of symptoms to COVID-19 versus baseline conditions was not handled correctly.
correct_approach: The analysis should have established each child's individual baseline symptom profile before infection and then assessed only new symptoms or meaningful worsening of pre-existing symptoms as post-COVID-19 outcomes. Symptoms that were already present and recurring at the same frequency before infection should have been excluded from the post-COVID-19 symptom count or modeled as time-varying covariates. The survival analysis should have been conditioned on the onset of truly new or exacerbated symptoms post-infection, rather than treating all reported symptoms post-infection as COVID-19-related. A control group of children who were not infected (or a comparison of symptom trajectories before and after infection within the same children, accounting for baseline rates) would have been essential to properly attribute symptoms to SARS-CoV-2.
additional_errors: The PCC definition used requires symptoms persisting for 8 weeks, but the WHO definition specifies symptoms lasting at least 2 months (approximately 8 weeks) starting 3 months after infection — the paper appears to conflate the persistence window with the post-infection onset window, potentially misapplying the WHO criteria.

Only 1 out of 271 children met PCC criteria (0.4%), yet the study reports detailed Cox regression hazard ratios with very narrow confidence intervals for symptom resolution, which seems inconsistent with such a small number of PCC cases — suggesting the survival analysis was run on all children with any post-COVID symptoms rather than just PCC cases, conflating acute illness recovery with long COVID.
The implausibly extreme hazard ratios (HR = 0.00 with CI 0.00-0.02 for cough) suggest model overfitting, separation, or fundamental misspecification in the Cox regression.

The study claims to follow STROBE guidelines but does not clearly report the number of children who tested positive for COVID-19, the total person-time at risk, or the number of events in the survival analysis, making the results difficult to evaluate.

Resolution of symptoms was defined as 4 weeks without symptoms (which can be nonconsecutive), which is an unusual and potentially problematic definition that could misclassify intermittent chronic symptoms as resolved and then recurrent.
